citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
On March 30th, 2026, two VMOs observed the following animals in indoor enclosures:
- A young brown and white rabbit appeared lethargic. The VMOs observed the rabbit had a very poor body condition (the
spine and ribs could be felt and little muscle was present). It did not react when the inspector touched it and was weak
when picked up by an employee. Diarrhea was visible around its tail. The inspection was paused so that the rabbit could
receive prompt treatment.
- Another young grey long-haired rabbit in an adjacent cage appeared to have similar signs. The animal was quiet, but
responsive, and was thin. Fecal staining was present on its fur and rear end.
- One adult parakeet was observed in the wellness room under observation for abnormal feathering, but was not receiving
any treatments. The parakeet was standing on the bottom of the cage, leaning forward. He was not able to walk without
using his beak to pull him forward.
- One adult cockatiel was observed in the quarantine room with similar symptoms. He was observed fluffed up at the
bottom of the cage and leaning forward, resting on his ankles instead of his feet. He was able to walk several steps but
appeared unbalanced.
The inspectors observed records demonstrating the facility had completed their morning daily observations before the
inspection. The facility had not observed or identified these conditions before the inspection. These observed animals
were not receiving any treatments or special care relative to their medical conditions.
Failing to perform daily observation of all animals and identify these conditions in a timely manner has prevented these
animals from receiving timely veterinary care.
The facility took measures to initiate treatment on these animals during the inspection. These animals' conditions must
communicated to and receive evaluation from a veterinarian. The facility must ensure a method of daily observation of all
animals to assess their health and well-being. The facility must ensure a mechanism for direct and frequent
communication to the attending veterinarian so that timely and accurate information on problems of animal health,
behavior, and well-being is conveyed.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
Additional Inspectors:
Alexandra Friedman, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3)  Direct Repeat
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
On March 30th, 2026, two VMOs observed the following animals in indoor enclosures: 
 
- A young brown and white rabbit appeared lethargic. The VMOs observed the rabbit had a very poor body condition (the 
spine and ribs could be felt and little muscle was present). It did not react when the inspector touched it and was weak 
when picked up by an employee. Diarrhea was visible around its tail. The inspection was paused so that the rabbit could 
receive prompt treatment. 
- Another young grey long-haired rabbit in an adjacent cage appeared to have similar signs. The animal was quiet, but 
responsive, and was thin. Fecal staining was present on its fur and rear end. 
- One adult parakeet was observed in the wellness room under observation for abnormal feathering, but was not receiving 
any treatments. The parakeet was standing on the bottom of the cage, leaning forward. He was not able to walk without 
using his beak to pull him forward. 
- One adult cockatiel was observed in the quarantine room with similar symptoms. He was observed fluffed up at the 
bottom of the cage and leaning forward, resting on his ankles instead of his feet. He was able to walk several steps but 
appeared unbalanced.  
 
The inspectors observed records demonstrating the facility had completed their morning daily observations before the 
inspection. The facility had not observed or identified these conditions before the inspection. These observed animals 
were not receiving any treatments or special care relative to their medical conditions. 
 
Failing to perform daily observation of all animals and identify these conditions in a timely manner has prevented these 
animals from receiving timely veterinary care. 
 
The facility took measures to initiate treatment on these animals during the inspection. These animals' conditions must 
communicated to and receive evaluation from a veterinarian. The facility must ensure a method of daily observation of all 
animals to assess their health and well-being. The facility must ensure a mechanism for direct and frequent 
communication to the attending veterinarian so that timely and accurate information on problems of animal health, 
behavior, and well-being is conveyed.

This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
Alexandra Friedman, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
